name: Apple Watch
short_description: A wrist-worn smartwatch that fuses health and motion sensing, on-device intelligence, and a voice assistant into an always-on body-worn device.
long_description: Announced in 2014 and released in 2015, the Apple Watch is a wrist-worn computer with optical heart-rate, accelerometer, gyroscope, and (in later models) ECG, blood-oxygen, and temperature sensors, plus the Siri voice assistant and increasingly on-device machine learning. It matters for physical AI because it is one of the most widely worn sensing-and-inference platforms in daily life: it continuously reads the body, runs models for fall detection, irregular-rhythm and crash detection, and activity coaching, and nudges the wearer through ambient haptics and notifications. As such it exemplifies how AI becomes legible not as a robot but as a quiet, always-present body-worn agent.

images.caption: An Apple Watch Series 7 with a digital watch face.
images.alt_text: An Apple Watch with a rounded-square screen showing a clock face, worn on a wrist.

sources.title: Apple Watch
sources.access_notes: Confirms announcement September 2014 and release April 24, 2015, plus sensor and feature history.

interactions.title: It calls for help when you go still
interactions.description: After a hard fall it taps your wrist and starts a countdown; if you don't move for about a minute it dials emergency services on its own and reads out your location. People have been pulled off cliffsides and bathroom floors this way.

interactions.title: A tap on the wrist that says 'see a cardiologist'
interactions.description: Between glances at the time, it quietly samples your pulse in the background and, if several readings look irregular, warns you of a rhythm that may be atrial fibrillation. For many wearers it's the first hint of a heart condition they had no symptoms of.

interactions.title: It listens to you breathe while you sleep
interactions.description: Worn to bed, it uses the accelerometer to track how your body moves with each breath, and after weeks of disturbed nights it hands you a notification — and a PDF to take to a doctor — suggesting possible sleep apnea.

interactions.title: Press your wrist to talk to someone far away
interactions.description: Hold the talk button and speak, and your voice crackles out of a relative's watch across the country a second later, like a walkie-talkie with no range limit — parents and kids use it to check in without dialing.

interactions.title: The rollercoaster that dials 911
interactions.description: The same accelerometer that catches car crashes can't tell a thrill ride from a wreck. Riders at Kings Island and Six Flags have stepped off coasters to find their watch had already auto-called emergency dispatchers, flooding park-area 911 lines with phantom crashes.

interactions.title: Pacing the living room at 11:45 to close the rings
interactions.description: The three daily rings turn movement into a streak you can break, and some wearers describe walking laps of an airport or shaking their wrist in class to avoid losing it — a few have ditched the watch entirely to escape the pressure.

interactions.title: Notifications you feel instead of hear
interactions.description: Deaf and hard-of-hearing wearers turn up the Taptic Engine so a text or a doorbell arrives as a distinct pattern of taps on the wrist — a quiet channel that works in a loud room or when the phone is buried in a bag. Grounded in the watch's haptic engine and always-on body contact.

interactions.title: A nudge to stand before the meeting drags on
interactions.description: Reading its accelerometer and your calendar, the watch could time its hourly stand reminder to land in the gap between back-to-back meetings rather than mid-sentence — using the motion sensing and on-device timing it already runs.
